file.content:
site_name: Rune Documentation
site_description: Implementation plan and architecture documentation for Rune
site_author: Noah
site_dir: site/docs
docs_dir: docs
exclude_docs: '**/node_modules/**

  **/.pytest_cache/**

  **/.ruff_cache/**

  **/.mypy_cache/**

  **/__pycache__/**

  **/src/**

  **/tests/**

  **/scripts/**

  **/alembic/**

  **/e2e/**

  **/dist/**

  **/.vite/**

  '
theme:
  name: mkdocs
  navigation_depth: 3
  locale: en
  logo: assets/et_illustration_transparent.png
  favicon: assets/et_illustration_transparent.png
extra_css:
- assets/custom.css
- assets/academic.css
extra_javascript:
- assets/mermaid-config.js
- https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mermaid@11/dist/mermaid.min.js
- assets/mermaid-loader.js
- assets/mathjax-config.js
- https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-chtml.js
plugins:
- search
- monorepo
- mkdocstrings:
    default_handler: python
    handlers:
      python:
        paths:
        - services/*/src
        - libs/*/src
        options:
          show_source: true
          show_root_heading: true
          show_category_heading: true
          show_signature_annotations: true
          show_bases: true
          heading_level: 2
          members_order: source
          docstring_style: google
          filters:
          - '!^_'
          merge_init_into_class: true
          show_if_no_docstring: false
          separate_signature: true
          signature_crossrefs: true
          show_submodules: true
          show_inheritance_diagram: true
          show_root_toc_entry: true
markdown_extensions:
- admonition
- pymdownx.details
- pymdownx.superfences:
    custom_fences:
    - name: mermaid
      class: mermaid
      format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_code_format ''
- pymdownx.tabbed:
    alternate_style: true
- tables
- toc:
    permalink: true
- pymdownx.arithmatex:
    generic: true
    inline_syntax:
    - round
    block_syntax:
    - square
    - begin
- footnotes
- attr_list
- md_in_html
validation:
  omitted_files: warn
  absolute_links: warn
  unrecognized_links: warn
  anchors: warn
nav:
- Home: index.md
- Implementation Plan: implementation-plan.md
- Architecture:
  - Recursive Loop: architecture/recursive-loop.md
  - Monorepo Mapping: architecture/monorepo-mapping.md
  - Adapter Storage: architecture/adapter-storage.md
  - Multi-GPU Strategy: architecture/multi-gpu-strategy.md
- Appendices:
  - Risk Matrix: appendices/risk-matrix.md
  - Build Order: appendices/build-order.md
- Scientific Article:
  - Overview: article/index.md
  - Abstract: article/abstract.md
  - Background: article/background.md
  - Methods: article/methods.md
  - Results: article/results.md
  - Discussion: article/discussion.md
  - References: article/references.md
- Components:
  - Overview: components-overview.md
  - adapter-registry: '!include ./libs/adapter-registry/mkdocs.yml'
  - api-service: '!include ./services/api-service/mkdocs.yml'
  - evaluation: '!include ./libs/evaluation/mkdocs.yml'
  - events-py: '!include ./libs/events-py/mkdocs.yml'
  - evolution-svc: '!include ./services/evolution-svc/mkdocs.yml'
  - inference: '!include ./libs/inference/mkdocs.yml'
  - model-training: '!include ./libs/model-training/mkdocs.yml'
  - rune-agent: '!include ./services/rune-agent/mkdocs.yml'
  - shared: '!include ./libs/shared/mkdocs.yml'
  - training-svc: '!include ./services/training-svc/mkdocs.yml'

content.thinking:
The issue is with how `pymdownx.superfences` generates the mermaid HTML and how the mermaid loader selects elements. Looking at the mkdocs.yml config: ```yaml - pymdownx.superfences: custom_fences: - name: mermaid class: mermaid format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_code_format '' ``` The `fence_code_format` formatter wraps the content in `
...
`. But mermaid.js expects the diagram definition to be inside an element with class `mermaid` directly, typically a `
` or `

The issue is likely in how `mermaid.run()` handles the selector and replaces the content.

The real problem is that mermaid can't properly render SVG inside `

` instead, which will let mermaid replace it with the actual SVG properly. the querySelector in mermaid-loader.js to match `div.mermaid`

- `fence_code_format` - wraps in `
`
- `fence_div_format` - wraps in `
`

Yes, `fence_div_format` exists.

Actually, the real standard solution for mermaid with pymdownx.superfences is to use `fence_div_format`, which generates `
` elements that mermaid.js can render directly. With `fence_code_format` instead, the content ends up nested in `
`, and mermaid tries to render the SVG inside the `` element, which causes layout issues. I need to update mkdocs.yml to use the div format and simplify the mermaid-loader.js accordingly, though I should double-check whether the current selector is actually matching the code element correctly when using `fence_code_format`.

Actually, I'm overthinking this. The symptom is clear: CSS is rendering as text, which means the SVG is being rendered inside a `` element. The fix is to switch to `fence_div_format` so mermaid renders into a proper `
` container instead.

The plan is straightforward: switch the formatter to `fence_div_format` in mkdocs.yml and update the mermaid loader to target `div.mermaid` elements. I need to switch from `fence_code_format` to `fence_div_format` in the mkdocs config and update the mermaid loader to target `div.mermaid` instead, since mermaid v11 expects the diagram content directly in a div without the extra code wrapper. I'm also realizing that mermaid v11 requires calling `mermaid.initialize()` rather than relying on `window.mermaidConfig`, and the auto-start behavior might still be triggering despite the `startOnLoad: false` setting. The real issue is that `fence_code_format` wraps mermaid diagrams in `
` tags, which prevents mermaid from rendering them properly—they need to be in `
` instead. Plus, `window.mermaidConfig` doesn't actually stop mermaid from auto-initializing on load in v11, so I need to either switch to `fence_div_format` or handle the initialization differently, possibly using an ES module import to control when mermaid starts.

Since they're using the UMD build, it auto-initializes on DOMContentLoaded and looks for `.mermaid` elements, but finds them wrapped in `` tags which breaks rendering. The cleanest fix is to change the fence format in mkdocs.yml, update the loader to work with the new structure, and properly prevent auto-init in the config file.

I'm considering setting `window.mermaid = { startOnLoad: false }` before mermaid loads, though that might not work with v11. Actually, the real issue is just the `fence_code_format` wrapping—if I switch to `fence_div_format` instead, mermaid's auto-init should handle everything without needing a custom loader at all.

But to be safe and ensure the dotted ID fix works, I'll keep the loader approach.

Writing the implementation...

content.text: The problem is `fence_code_format` wraps mermaid content in `<pre><code>` which can't properly contain the SVG output. It needs `fence_div_format` to produce `<div class="mermaid">` elements instead.

content.text:
Three changes made:

1. **mkdocs.yml**: `fence_code_format` → `fence_div_format` — mermaid content now renders in `<div class="mermaid">` instead of `<pre><code>`, which can properly contain SVG output.
2. **mermaid-config.js**: Set `window.mermaid = { startOnLoad: false }` so mermaid v11 actually reads the flag before auto-initializing.
3. **mermaid-loader.js**: Updated selectors to `.mermaid` to match the new div elements.

Rebuild the docs and the diagrams should render properly.

content.thinking:
The issue is clear now. The HTML output has `-->` instead of `-->`. The `fence_div_format` is HTML-escaping the content, turning `-->` into `-->`, which breaks mermaid's arrow syntax.

With `fence_code_format`, the content inside `<code>` is treated as code and may not be escaped the same way (or mermaid can handle it inside code elements). But `fence_div_format` escapes HTML entities in the div content.

I need a custom format function that doesn't HTML-escape the content. The typical solution for mermaid with pymdownx.superfences is to use a custom formatter. Let me write one.


I should create a custom fence format function that wraps the mermaid source in a `<pre class="mermaid">` tag without HTML-escaping, which is how mkdocs-material and other tools handle this. I see the issue now—both formatting approaches escape HTML entities, which breaks mermaid's parser when it sees `-->` instead of `-->`. I need to create a custom formatter that outputs the raw mermaid source without escaping. I'll write a Python module that defines this formatter and reference it in mkdocs.yml, but I'm realizing there might be a configuration issue with how to properly import it. I'm verifying that `<pre class="mermaid">` with raw, unescaped content will work properly—mermaid handles this format fine, and the diagram syntax doesn't typically contain HTML special characters that would cause issues. Now I'll write the custom formatter function.

content.text: The problem is `fence_div_format` HTML-escapes the content — `-->` becomes `-->` which breaks mermaid's arrow syntax. I need a custom formatter that outputs raw content.

input.content:
"""Custom fence formatter for mermaid diagrams. pymdownx.superfences' built-in formatters HTML-escape content, which turns mermaid arrows (-->) into --> and breaks parsing. This formatter outputs raw source inside a 
 block, which is exactly what
mermaid.js expects.
"""


def fence_mermaid_format(source, language, class_name, options, md, **kwargs):
    return f'
{source}
'

content.thinking:
Hmm, mkdocs can't find the module. This is because `uv run` might have a different Python path. Let me check how mkdocs resolves Python modules. The issue might be that the project root isn't in `sys.path` when running via `uv run`.

Let me try a different approach - I can add the current directory to the path, or use a different location. Actually, the common approach is to use a `hooks` directory or add the module as a plugin hook. But the simplest thing might be to check if there's a way to make it importable.

Actually, let me check if `PYTHONPATH` helps, or if I should add the file as part of a package.
 
Wait, I'm realizing the pymdownx documentation says the custom format function needs to be importable, and when using `uv run mkdocs`, the current working directory might not be in `sys.path`. Let me try setting `PYTHONPATH=.` to see if that helps. Actually, maybe I should skip the custom Python formatter entirely and handle this in JavaScript instead, since the original fence code format was already processing mermaid diagrams.

The real issue was that mermaid was rendering the SVG, but it was getting wrapped inside `<code>` tags which made the CSS display as text. What if I use the JavaScript loader to extract the content from the `<code>` element and move it to a plain `<div>` before mermaid processes it? That way the SVG won't be constrained by code block styling. Let me reconsider the HTML structure that fence_code_format actually produces—it depends on the pymdownx.superfences version, so I need to check what we're actually working with. So mermaid actually parsed the diagram successfully—it generated the CSS for the SVG—but the rendered output was trapped inside the `<code>` element as text. The real problem isn't the HTML escaping, it's that mermaid needs to render into a `<div>`, not a `<code>` block. I could either move the mermaid content from `<code>` to `<div>` in the JavaScript loader, or try setting the PYTHONPATH to make the custom formatter work properly. Let me start with the PYTHONPATH approach since it's more straightforward.

input.command: PYTHONPATH=. uv run mkdocs build 2>&1 | tail -5

content.thinking:
So the `.mermaid` element already contains rendered SVG text content (the CSS styles from the SVG). This means mermaid auto-initialized and rendered inside the `<pre class="mermaid">` element before my loader script ran. Then when my loader tries to run, it finds the already-rendered content (SVG CSS) instead of the original mermaid source code.

The issue is that mermaid's CDN script auto-initializes on load and finds `<pre class="mermaid">` elements. Even though I set `window.mermaid = { startOnLoad: false }` in the config script, the mermaid CDN script overwrites that when it loads.


The real problem is the load order: my config sets `startOnLoad: false`, but then the mermaid library loads and completely replaces that object, triggering auto-initialization on DOMContentLoaded before my loader script even gets a chance to run. Since mermaid v11 doesn't respect the pre-existing config, I need a different approach.

Rather than fighting the auto-init, I could preprocess the DOM with a synchronous script that runs before the mermaid CDN loads, transforming the malformed `<pre class="mermaid"><code>` structure into something mermaid can actually render correctly.

But wait—the HTML entities are escaped in the source, and when mermaid reads `textContent` from the `<pre>`, it gets the unescaped text. The real issue is that `<code>` wrapper nested inside the `<pre>` is interfering with how mermaid parses the diagram content.

Actually, let me think through this more carefully. When mermaid finds a `.mermaid` element and reads its `textContent`, it gets the decoded text without the HTML entities. So mermaid would correctly parse the flowchart syntax. After rendering, mermaid replaces the content with an SVG inside that same `<pre>` element. The problem might be that CSS styling on the `<pre>` tag is treating the SVG as preformatted text instead of rendering it as a graphic.

But wait—the screenshot actually shows mermaid did attempt to render something and threw a syntax error. If the HTML entities were being decoded properly, the syntax should be valid. So maybe the issue is that mermaid is reading the raw HTML entities as literal text instead of getting the decoded version, which would explain why it's failing to parse the diagram. be valid. Unless... mermaid is reading `innerHTML` instead of `textContent`? If it reads innerHTML, it would get `-->` which is indeed a syntax error.

Let me check: in mermaid v11, the `run()` function reads elements using... let me think. It likely reads `textContent` or `innerText`. But maybe there's a difference.

Actually, looking at this from a different angle. My loader script ran, found `code.language-mermaid` elements, read their `textContent` (which would be decoded), created divs, and called `mermaid.run()`. BUT - the snapshot shows "Syntax error in text", which means mermaid couldn't parse the content.

So the real issue might be that mermaid auto-initializes before my loader runs, finds the `<pre class="mermaid">` elements with `<code>` children, and reads the content - but something about that structure or the way it reads the text is causing the syntax error. Let me look at the actual snapshot to see what's happening. I see the issue now—mermaid is reading the `innerHTML` instead of `textContent`, which means it's seeing the raw HTML tags and encoded entities like `-->` instead of the actual arrow syntax. That's what's triggering the syntax error. To fix this, I need to disable mermaid's automatic initialization so my loader script can extract the plain text content before mermaid tries to parse it.

Looking at how mermaid v11 handles auto-initialization, it checks for the `startOnLoad` option when the script loads. The tricky part is that the UMD build overwrites the `window.mermaid` object when it initializes, so I need to figure out how to set `startOnLoad: false` before that happens. I'm reconsidering the approach—maybe I should go back to using `PYTHONPATH=.` with the custom formatter, or find a way to embed it differently. Using mkdocs hooks to add the module path could work, or I could leverage `uv run` to ensure the project root is properly on Python's path. Another option is to stick with `fence_div_format` and handle the HTML entity decoding in the JavaScript loader instead of trying to escape it server-side.

Actually, I think I've found the real solution: preprocess the DOM synchronously before mermaid loads. I'll create a config script that runs before the mermaid CDN loads and handles the div transformation...

      - paragraph [ref=e66]: "The recursive loop is Rune's central execution mechanism: a task enters, code is generated, executed, evaluated, and — if tests fail — the trajectory feeds back into generation. When the loop terminates (tests pass or max attempts reached), the complete trajectory is distilled into a LoRA adapter. This document specifies the data flow at each step, retry logic, and termination conditions."

content.text:
All mermaid diagrams are rendering correctly across pages. Here's a summary of what was done:

**Root cause:** Two issues — `fence_code_format` wraps content in `<pre><code>` (SVG can't render inside `<code>`), and mermaid's CDN auto-initializes before any preprocessing can happen, reading HTML-escaped arrows (`-->`) as syntax errors.

**Fix:** A single `mermaid-loader.js` that:
1. Extracts decoded text from `<code>` elements, strips the `<code>` wrapper
2. Dynamically loads mermaid *after* preprocessing, preventing the auto-init race
3. Calls `mermaid.run()` with full control over timing

**Files changed:**
- `mkdocs.yml` — removed `mermaid-config.js` and CDN script from `extra_javascript`, kept only `mermaid-loader.js`
- `docs/assets/mermaid-loader.js` — rewritten to handle preprocessing + dynamic loading
- `docs/assets/mermaid-config.js` — deleted (no longer needed)

    - main [ref=e72]:
      - heading "Multi-GPU Strategy" [level=1] [ref=e73]
      - heading "Overview" [level=2] [ref=e74]
      - paragraph [ref=e75]: Rune runs on 2x NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs (24 GB VRAM each, 48 GB total) connected via CXL. This document specifies the required multi-GPU configuration — pipeline parallelism with PP=2 and TP=1 — explains why tensor parallelism is excluded, and documents the GPU lease mechanism that coordinates concurrent training and serving workloads.
      - paragraph [ref=e76]:
        - text: For the services that use these GPUs, see
        - link "Monorepo Mapping" [ref=e77] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: ../monorepo-mapping/
        - text: . For the adapter format served by the GPU layer, see
        - link "Adapter Storage" [ref=e78] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: ../adapter-storage/
        - text: .
      - separator [ref=e79]
      - heading "Required Configuration" [level=2] [ref=e80]
      - table [ref=e81]:
        - rowgroup [ref=e82]:
          - row "Parameter Value Rationale" [ref=e83]:
            - columnheader "Parameter" [ref=e84]
            - columnheader "Value" [ref=e85]
            - columnheader "Rationale" [ref=e86]
        - rowgroup [ref=e87]:
          - row "--pipeline-parallel-size 2 Splits model layers across both GPUs; passes activations at layer boundaries only" [ref=e88]:
            - cell "--pipeline-parallel-size" [ref=e89]:
              - code [ref=e90]: "--pipeline-parallel-size"
            - cell "2" [ref=e91]
            - cell "Splits model layers across both GPUs; passes activations at layer boundaries only" [ref=e92]
          - row "--tensor-parallel-size 1 Disabled. See \"Why No Tensor Parallelism\" below" [ref=e93]:
            - cell "--tensor-parallel-size" [ref=e94]:
              - code [ref=e95]: "--tensor-parallel-size"
            - cell "1" [ref=e96]
            - cell "Disabled. See \"Why No Tensor Parallelism\" below" [ref=e97]
          - row "--enable-lora true Required for dynamic adapter loading via S-LoRA unified paging" [ref=e98]:
            - cell "--enable-lora" [ref=e99]:
              - code [ref=e100]: "--enable-lora"
            - cell "true" [ref=e101]
            - cell "Required for dynamic adapter loading via S-LoRA unified paging" [ref=e102]
          - row "--quantization awq or gptq (serving) 4-bit quantized serving for VRAM headroom" [ref=e103]:
            - cell "--quantization" [ref=e104]:
              - code [ref=e105]: "--quantization"
            - cell "awq or gptq (serving)" [ref=e106]
            - cell "4-bit quantized serving for VRAM headroom" [ref=e107]
          - row "Base model Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct 7B parameter SLM; fits in 24 GB per GPU with quantization" [ref=e108]:
            - cell "Base model" [ref=e109]
            - cell "Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct" [ref=e110]
            - cell "7B parameter SLM; fits in 24 GB per GPU with quantization" [ref=e111]
      - heading "Pipeline Parallelism (PP=2)" [level=3] [ref=e112]
      - paragraph [ref=e113]: Pipeline parallelism splits the transformer layers across GPUs. GPU 0 holds layers 0 through N/2, GPU 1 holds layers N/2+1 through N. During a forward pass, activations are passed once at the layer boundary — a single point-to-point transfer per request, not per layer.
      - document [ref=e115]:
        - generic [ref=e121]:
          - paragraph [ref=e130]: Token Input
          - paragraph [ref=e137]: "GPU 0: Layers 0 to N/2"
          - paragraph [ref=e144]: Activation Transfer via PCIe
          - paragraph [ref=e151]: "GPU 1: Layers N/2+1 to N"
          - paragraph [ref=e160]: Token Output
      - paragraph [ref=e161]: The PCIe bandwidth (~32 GB/s bidirectional) is sufficient for this pattern because the transfer happens once per forward pass at a single layer boundary. Activation tensors at a layer boundary for a 7B model are small relative to the bus bandwidth.
      - separator [ref=e162]
      - heading "Why No Tensor Parallelism" [level=2] [ref=e163]
      - paragraph [ref=e164]:
        - text: Tensor parallelism (TP) is explicitly excluded (
        - code [ref=e165]: "--tensor-parallel-size 1"
        - text: ). Three independent factors make TP non-viable on this hardware.
      - 'heading "Factor 1: No NVLink" [level=3] [ref=e166]'
      - paragraph [ref=e167]: "Tensor parallelism shards individual weight matrices across GPUs and requires all-reduce synchronization at every transformer layer. On NVLink (~112 GB/s per direction), this is fast. On PCIe (~32 GB/s bidirectional), the all-reduce at every layer becomes the bottleneck:"
      - table [ref=e168]:
        - rowgroup [ref=e169]:
          - row "Interconnect Bandwidth All-Reduce Overhead per Layer Viable for TP?" [ref=e170]:
            - columnheader "Interconnect" [ref=e171]
            - columnheader "Bandwidth" [ref=e172]
            - columnheader "All-Reduce Overhead per Layer" [ref=e173]
            - columnheader "Viable for TP?" [ref=e174]
        - rowgroup [ref=e175]:
          - row "NVLink (A100/H100) ~112 GB/s per direction Negligible Yes" [ref=e176]:
            - cell "NVLink (A100/H100)" [ref=e177]
            - cell "~112 GB/s per direction" [ref=e178]
            - cell "Negligible" [ref=e179]
            - cell "Yes" [ref=e180]
          - row "PCIe Gen4 (RTX 4090) ~32 GB/s bidirectional Significant No" [ref=e181]:
            - cell "PCIe Gen4 (RTX 4090)" [ref=e182]
            - cell "~32 GB/s bidirectional" [ref=e183]
            - cell "Significant" [ref=e184]
            - cell "No" [ref=e185]
      - paragraph [ref=e186]: The RTX 4090 does not have NVLink. PCIe is the only available interconnect for GPU-to-GPU communication. Pipeline parallelism avoids this bottleneck by transferring activations once at a layer boundary instead of synchronizing at every layer.
      - 'heading "Factor 2: vLLM TP+LoRA Bug (#21471)" [level=3] [ref=e187]'
      - paragraph [ref=e188]:
        - text: vLLM issue
        - link "#21471" [ref=e189] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/21471
        - text: documents a confirmed bug where tensor parallelism combined with LoRA adapter serving produces corrupted outputs on consumer GPUs without NVLink. The bug manifests as silently wrong generated text — not crashes or errors — making it particularly dangerous. The corruption is absent under pipeline parallelism (PP=2, TP=1), which is the confirmed working configuration.
      - 'heading "Factor 3: CXL Interconnect Characteristics" [level=3] [ref=e190]'
      - paragraph [ref=e191]: The CXL interconnect between the two GPUs provides cache-coherent memory pooling, which benefits CPU-GPU coordination and shared metadata access. However, CXL does not provide the low-latency, high-bandwidth GPU-to-GPU data path that tensor parallelism requires. CXL is designed for memory expansion and coherence, not for the tight synchronization pattern of all-reduce operations.

      - paragraph [ref=e232]: The 7B model in NF4 quantization is approximately 4 GB total, split across both GPUs. S-LoRA unified paging manages adapter weights alongside KV cache in a shared GPU memory pool, enabling concurrent serving of multiple adapters without per-adapter VRAM reservation.
      - heading "Training Mode (single GPU)" [level=3] [ref=e233]

      - paragraph [ref=e262]: "QLoRA is required for training: the base model is frozen in NF4 4-bit, and gradients flow through the quantized weights into bf16 LoRA adapter matrices. Without QLoRA, a 7B model in bf16 (~14 GB) leaves insufficient headroom for optimizer states and activations on a 24 GB GPU."
      - separator [ref=e263]
      - heading "GPU Lease Mechanism" [level=2] [ref=e264]
      - paragraph [ref=e265]: The lora-server (vLLM, PP=2) normally occupies both GPUs for inference. When training-svc needs GPU time for fine-tuning or hypernetwork training, a lease mechanism coordinates the handoff.

      - heading "Design Decisions" [level=3] [ref=e362]
      - paragraph [ref=e363]:
        - strong [ref=e364]: Why not dedicate one GPU per workload permanently?
        - text: At 24 GB per GPU, a permanently split configuration leaves insufficient VRAM for either workload to run optimally. PP=2 serving uses the full 48 GB for KV cache and adapter paging. Training needs a full GPU for activations and optimizer states. Time-sharing via the lease mechanism gives each workload full resources during its active period.
      - paragraph [ref=e365]:
        - strong [ref=e366]: Why does lora-server yield, not training-svc queue indefinitely?
        - text: Inference is latency-sensitive but interruptible between requests. Training jobs run for minutes to hours. The lease mechanism prioritizes training throughput (no VRAM competition) while keeping inference available in degraded mode on the remaining GPU.
      - paragraph [ref=e367]:
        - strong [ref=e368]: Lease coordination is implemented via a shared state file
        - text: at
        - code [ref=e369]: ~/.rune/gpu_lease.json
        - text: ", protected by file locking. The lora-server polls this file between request batches. The state file contains:"

        - text: (service name or null),
        - code [ref=e371]: granted_at
        - text: (timestamp),
        - code [ref=e372]: gpu_id
        - text: (which GPU is leased), and
        - code [ref=e373]: expires_at
        - text: (maximum lease duration, default 2 hours).

      - paragraph [ref=e403]: CXL provides cache-coherent memory pooling across both GPUs, which benefits the adapter registry (shared metadata access) and coordination between serving and training processes. It does not substitute for NVLink in GPU-to-GPU compute synchronization.

    - main [ref=e72]:
      - heading "Multi-GPU Strategy" [level=1] [ref=e73]
      - heading "Overview" [level=2] [ref=e74]
      - paragraph [ref=e75]: Rune runs on 2x NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs (24 GB VRAM each, 48 GB total) connected via CXL. This document specifies the required multi-GPU configuration — pipeline parallelism with PP=2 and TP=1 — explains why tensor parallelism is excluded, and documents the GPU lease mechanism that coordinates concurrent training and serving workloads.
      - paragraph [ref=e76]:
        - text: For the services that use these GPUs, see
        - link "Monorepo Mapping" [ref=e77] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: ../monorepo-mapping/
        - text: . For the adapter format served by the GPU layer, see

        - text: .
      - separator [ref=e79]
      - heading "Required Configuration" [level=2] [ref=e80]
      - table [ref=e81]:
        - rowgroup [ref=e82]:
          - row "Parameter Value Rationale" [ref=e83]:

            - columnheader "Value" [ref=e85]
            - columnheader "Rationale" [ref=e86]
        - rowgroup [ref=e87]:
          - row "--pipeline-parallel-size 2 Splits model layers across both GPUs; passes activations at layer boundaries only" [ref=e88]:
            - cell "--pipeline-parallel-size" [ref=e89]:

            - cell "2" [ref=e91]

            - cell "true" [ref=e101]
            - cell "Required for dynamic adapter loading via S-LoRA unified paging" [ref=e102]
          - row "--quantization awq or gptq (serving) 4-bit quantized serving for VRAM headroom" [ref=e103]:
            - cell "--quantization" [ref=e104]:

          - row "Base model Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct 7B parameter SLM; fits in 24 GB per GPU with quantization" [ref=e108]:
            - cell "Base model" [ref=e109]
            - cell "Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct" [ref=e110]
            - cell "7B parameter SLM; fits in 24 GB per GPU with quantization" [ref=e111]
      - heading "Pipeline Parallelism (PP=2)" [level=3] [ref=e112]
      - paragraph [ref=e113]: Pipeline parallelism splits the transformer layers across GPUs. GPU 0 holds layers 0 through N/2, GPU 1 holds layers N/2+1 through N. During a forward pass, activations are passed once at the layer boundary — a single point-to-point transfer per request, not per layer.
      - document [ref=e115]:
        - generic [ref=e121]:
          - paragraph [ref=e130]: Token Input
          - paragraph [ref=e137]: "GPU 0: Layers 0 to N/2"
          - paragraph [ref=e144]: Activation Transfer via PCIe
          - paragraph [ref=e151]: "GPU 1: Layers N/2+1 to N"
          - paragraph [ref=e160]: Token Output
      - paragraph [ref=e161]: The PCIe bandwidth (~32 GB/s bidirectional) is sufficient for this pattern because the transfer happens once per forward pass at a single layer boundary. Activation tensors at a layer boundary for a 7B model are small relative to the bus bandwidth.
      - separator [ref=e162]
      - heading "Why No Tensor Parallelism" [level=2] [ref=e163]
      - paragraph [ref=e164]:
        - text: Tensor parallelism (TP) is explicitly excluded (
        - code [ref=e165]: "--tensor-parallel-size 1"
        - text: ). Three independent factors make TP non-viable on this hardware.
      - 'heading "Factor 1: No NVLink" [level=3] [ref=e166]'
      - paragraph [ref=e167]: "Tensor parallelism shards individual weight matrices across GPUs and requires all-reduce synchronization at every transformer layer. On NVLink (~112 GB/s per direction), this is fast. On PCIe (~32 GB/s bidirectional), the all-reduce at every layer becomes the bottleneck:"
      - table [ref=e168]:
        - rowgroup [ref=e169]:
          - row "Interconnect Bandwidth All-Reduce Overhead per Layer Viable for TP?" [ref=e170]:
            - columnheader "Interconnect" [ref=e171]
            - columnheader "Bandwidth" [ref=e172]
            - columnheader "All-Reduce Overhead per Layer" [ref=e173]
            - columnheader "Viable for TP?" [ref=e174]
        - rowgroup [ref=e175]:
          - row "NVLink (A100/H100) ~112 GB/s per direction Negligible Yes" [ref=e176]:
            - cell "NVLink (A100/H100)" [ref=e177]
            - cell "~112 GB/s per direction" [ref=e178]
            - cell "Negligible" [ref=e179]
            - cell "Yes" [ref=e180]
          - row "PCIe Gen4 (RTX 4090) ~32 GB/s bidirectional Significant No" [ref=e181]:
            - cell "PCIe Gen4 (RTX 4090)" [ref=e182]
            - cell "~32 GB/s bidirectional" [ref=e183]
            - cell "Significant" [ref=e184]
            - cell "No" [ref=e185]
      - paragraph [ref=e186]: The RTX 4090 does not have NVLink. PCIe is the only available interconnect for GPU-to-GPU communication. Pipeline parallelism avoids this bottleneck by transferring activations once at a layer boundary instead of synchronizing at every layer.
      - 'heading "Factor 2: vLLM TP+LoRA Bug (#21471)" [level=3] [ref=e187]'
      - paragraph [ref=e188]:
        - text: vLLM issue
        - link "#21471" [ref=e189] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/21471
        - text: documents a confirmed bug where tensor parallelism combined with LoRA adapter serving produces corrupted outputs on consumer GPUs without NVLink. The bug manifests as silently wrong generated text — not crashes or errors — making it particularly dangerous. The corruption is absent under pipeline parallelism (PP=2, TP=1), which is the confirmed working configuration.
      - 'heading "Factor 3: CXL Interconnect Characteristics" [level=3] [ref=e190]'
      - paragraph [ref=e191]: The CXL interconnect between the two GPUs provides cache-coherent memory pooling, which benefits CPU-GPU coordination and shared metadata access. However, CXL does not provide the low-latency, high-bandwidth GPU-to-GPU data path that tensor parallelism requires. CXL is designed for memory expansion and coherence, not for the tight synchronization pattern of all-reduce operations.

      - paragraph [ref=e265]: The lora-server (vLLM, PP=2) normally occupies both GPUs for inference. When training-svc needs GPU time for fine-tuning or hypernetwork training, a lease mechanism coordinates the handoff.

      - paragraph [ref=e363]:

        - text: At 24 GB per GPU, a permanently split configuration leaves insufficient VRAM for either workload to run optimally. PP=2 serving uses the full 48 GB for KV cache and adapter paging. Training needs a full GPU for activations and optimizer states. Time-sharing via the lease mechanism gives each workload full resources during its active period.

    - main [ref=e120]:
      - heading "Rune Implementation Plan" [level=1] [ref=e121]
      - heading "Executive Summary" [level=2] [ref=e122]
      - paragraph [ref=e123]: Rune proposes to validate and implement a system that encodes coding trajectories into LoRA adapters using a Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork, accumulating parametric memory that persists across sessions. This plan covers the full journey from hardware validation through hypernetwork training across five implementation phases (Phase 0 through Phase 4), structured so that the core hypothesis is validated before infrastructure is built.

      - heading "Key Risks" [level=3] [ref=e153]
      - paragraph [ref=e154]:
        - text: Three primary research risks are tracked throughout this plan. See
        - link "Risk Matrix" [ref=e155] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: ../appendices/risk-matrix/
        - text: for full mitigation strategies and warning signs.
      - list [ref=e156]:
        - listitem [ref=e157]:
          - strong [ref=e158]: Hypernetwork mode collapse
          - text: — The degenerate solution (mean adapter) has near-zero variance across inputs; diversity regularization in the training loss is required from the start.
        - listitem [ref=e159]:
          - strong [ref=e160]: Adapter composition interference
          - text: — Direct additive merging of heterogeneous LoRAs produces interference in non-orthogonal subspaces; default to single-adapter retrieval.
        - listitem [ref=e161]:
          - strong [ref=e162]: Catastrophic forgetting
          - text: — Adapter registry must enforce write-once semantics; no code path may overwrite an existing adapter.
      - heading "Build Order" [level=3] [ref=e163]
      - paragraph [ref=e164]:
        - text: The recommended component build sequence is detailed in
        - link "Build Order" [ref=e165] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: ../appendices/build-order/
        - text: . The dependency root is
        - code [ref=e166]: libs/adapter-registry
        - text: — it must be built first, as all other components depend on it for adapter storage and retrieval.

      - heading "Research-Stage Framing" [level=3] [ref=e233]
      - paragraph [ref=e234]: "This plan is structured for a research-stage system: the phases after the kill-switch gate (Phase 2 through Phase 4) are contingent on the hypothesis validated in Phase 1. Writing them out does not imply confidence that they will be reached — it means the design is complete enough to proceed if the hypothesis holds. If Phase 1 fails, the plan does not prescribe a pivot. The failure itself is informative and narrows the design space."
      - separator [ref=e235]
      - 'heading "Phase 0: Hardware and Environment Validation" [level=2] [ref=e236]'
      - paragraph [ref=e237]:
        - strong [ref=e238]: "Depends on:"
        - text: Nothing — this is the first phase.
      - paragraph [ref=e239]: "Phase 0 confirms that the hardware and software environment is ready for GPU-dependent work. This is a precondition gate, not a research gate: none of the hypothesis testing in Phase 1 is possible without a validated environment. The success criteria are entirely binary — each check either passes or fails, with no partial credit."
      - heading "Why This Phase Comes First" [level=3] [ref=e240]
      - paragraph [ref=e241]: "GPU-dependent workloads fail in hardware-specific, non-obvious ways: CUDA version mismatches cause segfaults during backward passes, vLLM may require compilation from source for newer pipeline-parallelism features, and the TP+LoRA corruption bug (vLLM issue #21471) can produce silently wrong outputs rather than crashes. Discovering any of these mid-experiment invalidates results and forces a debugging detour. Phase 0 establishes a clean baseline before any research work begins."
      - paragraph [ref=e242]: The distinction between Phase 0 and an installation guide is intentional. Phase 0 validates that the environment works correctly — the pass/fail tests confirm behavior, not installation. The steps required to achieve a passing environment (installing drivers, compiling vLLM, configuring CUDA paths) are pre-conditions that precede this phase.

      - paragraph [ref=e269]:
        - strong [ref=e270]: "Note on tensor parallelism:"
        - code [ref=e271]: "--tensor-parallel-size 2"
        - text: "is explicitly excluded. All-reduce operations over PCIe (~32 GB/s) are prohibitively expensive compared to NVLink (~112 GB/s per direction). Pipeline parallelism passes activations only at layer boundaries and is the correct strategy for this interconnect. The TP+LoRA corruption bug (vLLM #21471) applies to this hardware configuration and is confirmed absent under PP=2."

      - separator [ref=e320]
      - 'heading "Phase 1: Core Hypothesis Validation (Kill-Switch Gate)" [level=2] [ref=e321]'
      - paragraph [ref=e322]:
        - strong [ref=e323]: "Depends on:"
        - text: Phase 0 passes all success criteria.
      - paragraph [ref=e324]: "Phase 1 tests the central hypothesis empirically: can a Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork encode coding trajectories into LoRA adapters that improve task performance over the base model? No infrastructure is built until this gate passes. The rationale for this sequencing is that the adapter registry, serving layer, and recursive agent loop are all contingent on the hypothesis holding — building them before validation would be premature optimization of infrastructure around an unvalidated core idea."
      - heading "Why This Phase Comes Before Infrastructure" [level=3] [ref=e325]
      - paragraph [ref=e326]: "The infrastructure-first instinct — build the registry, the serving layer, the agent loop, then test the hypothesis — is common and historically problematic. Building infrastructure before validating the core idea embeds assumptions into architecture that are difficult to reverse if the idea fails. The study of failed ML projects (RAND 2024) consistently identifies premature infrastructure investment as a compounding failure mode: teams that discover a hypothesis failure late have also accumulated technical debt in systems that depend on it."
      - paragraph [ref=e327]: "Rune's kill-switch gate is the operational expression of hypothesis-first ordering: Phase 2 through Phase 4 are real and detailed, but they are explicitly conditional. Passing Phase 1 is what makes them worth building."
      - heading "Deliverables" [level=3] [ref=e328]
      - list [ref=e329]:
        - listitem [ref=e330]: "[ ] Minimal Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork trained on 50-100 coding trajectory pairs (one trajectory per task: task description, attempt sequence, final passing code)"
        - listitem [ref=e331]: "[ ] Adapter quality evaluation on held-out HumanEval subset (20-30 tasks, 5 samples per task)"
        - listitem [ref=e332]:
          - text: "[ ] MLflow run documenting: Pass@1 vs baseline, training loss curve, adapter cosine diversity across training batch,"
          - code [ref=e333]: "||ΔW||"
          - text: norms
        - listitem [ref=e334]: "[ ] Written assessment: what passed, what failed, what was learned — regardless of whether the gate passes or fails"
      - heading "Kill-Switch" [level=3] [ref=e335]
      - blockquote [ref=e336]:
        - paragraph [ref=e337]:
          - strong [ref=e338]: "Primary metric:"
          - text: Pass@1 improvement of ≥ 5% on held-out HumanEval tasks (20-30 task subset) compared to Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct baseline with no adapter.
        - paragraph [ref=e339]:
          - strong [ref=e340]: "If the gate passes:"
          - text: The hypothesis has empirical support — not proof, but support. Proceed to Phase 2. The architectural decisions made in Phases 2 through 4 are now worth acting on.
        - paragraph [ref=e341]:
          - strong [ref=e342]: "If the gate fails:"
          - text: Stop and document what was learned. There is no predefined fallback strategy. The failure narrows the design space — it is a research finding, not a project failure. The specific failure mode (mode collapse, no adapter transfer, training instability, insufficient trajectory signal) determines what comes next. Prescribing a fallback now would be speculative.
      - paragraph [ref=e343]:
        - strong [ref=e344]: "Hardware note for Phase 1:"
        - text: Run the baseline in bfloat16 — not QLoRA. This isolates the hypothesis variable (does Doc-to-LoRA on coding trajectories work?) from the quantization variable (does NF4 quantization degrade adapter quality?). QLoRA is introduced in Phase 2 after the bfloat16 baseline passes.
      - heading "Secondary Diagnostic Signals" [level=3] [ref=e345]
      - paragraph [ref=e346]: "These are tracked via MLflow and inform the written assessment, but do not gate the kill-switch decision:"
      - list [ref=e347]:
        - listitem [ref=e348]: Adapter cosine diversity > 0.1 across training batch (early signal for mode collapse — if adapters are clustering, the hypernetwork is collapsing to the mean)
        - listitem [ref=e349]: Training loss converges and does not plateau in the first 5% of the budget (training is learning, not stuck)
        - listitem [ref=e350]:
          - code [ref=e351]: "||ΔW||"
          - text: is meaningfully nonzero (the adapter is affecting the base model's behavior, not being ignored)
      - heading "Risk Callouts" [level=3] [ref=e352]
      - blockquote [ref=e353]:
        - paragraph [ref=e354]:
          - strong [ref=e355]: Hypernetwork mode collapse
          - text: (see
          - link "Risk Matrix" [ref=e356] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: ../appendices/risk-matrix/
          - text: "): The hypernetwork may learn to produce near-identical adapters regardless of input trajectory — the degenerate solution. Diversity regularization in the training loss and monitoring of cosine diversity are the primary mitigations. If diversity collapses during Phase 1, the gate will fail (Pass@1 will not improve across different task types), but the diagnostic signals should identify the failure mode before the full evaluation."
        - paragraph [ref=e357]:
          - strong [ref=e358]: "Training data quality:"
          - text: Trajectories must be diverse in task type, failure mode, and correction pattern. A dataset of 50-100 nearly-identical trajectories (e.g., all variations on the same task) will produce mode collapse regardless of architecture. Verify trajectory diversity before training begins.

      - heading "Experiment Sketch" [level=3] [ref=e384]
      - paragraph [ref=e385]:
        - strong [ref=e386]: "Dataset:"
        - text: "50-100 real coding trajectory pairs from HumanEval or SWE-bench-lite. Each trajectory consists of: task description, attempt sequence (code + error messages + corrections), final passing code. Trajectories sourced from existing evaluation runs or synthetically generated via a prompted base model."
      - paragraph [ref=e387]:
        - strong [ref=e388]: "Baseline:"
        - text: Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct with no adapter — raw model Pass@1 on the held-out task subset (5 samples per task, bfloat16, PP=2 serving).
      - paragraph [ref=e389]:
        - strong [ref=e390]: "Evaluation method:"
        - text: Pass@1 on 20-30 held-out HumanEval tasks. Five samples per task. Tasks held out from the training trajectory corpus to test generalization, not memorization.
      - paragraph [ref=e391]:
        - strong [ref=e392]: "Expected range:"
        - text: 5-15% improvement over baseline signals a real effect and passes the gate. Less than 5% = gate fails. Greater than 15% should be treated skeptically and checked against the secondary diagnostics for data leakage.
      - paragraph [ref=e393]:
        - strong [ref=e394]: "Tracking:"
        - text: "MLflow experiment with schema:"
        - code [ref=e395]: run_id
        - text: ","

        - text: ","

        - text: ","

      - separator [ref=e404]
      - 'heading "Phase 2: Adapter Library and Serving Infrastructure" [level=2] [ref=e405]'
      - paragraph [ref=e406]:
        - strong [ref=e407]: "Depends on:"
        - text: Phase 1 kill-switch passes.
      - paragraph [ref=e408]:
        - text: "Assuming Phase 1 validates the core hypothesis, Phase 2 builds the component foundation: the adapter registry, vLLM serving with dynamic LoRA loading, API extensions, and QLoRA integration. These components are the dependency roots of the"
        - link "build order" [ref=e409] [cursor=pointer]:
          - /url: ../appendices/build-order/
        - text: — every subsequent phase depends on them.
      - heading "Why This Phase Comes Here" [level=3] [ref=e410]
      - paragraph [ref=e411]:
        - text: The adapter registry (
        - code [ref=e412]: libs/adapter-registry
        - text: ") is the foundation of the entire system: every component that stores or retrieves adapters depends on it. The vLLM lora-server ("
        - code [ref=e413]: services/lora-server
        - text: ) unblocks all agent work and hypothesis testing. Building these before Phase 3 or Phase 4 would have been premature if Phase 1 had failed — infrastructure built around an unvalidated hypothesis is wasted work. With Phase 1 passing, these components are now justified.
      - paragraph [ref=e414]: QLoRA is introduced in this phase (not Phase 1) to isolate variables. The Phase 1 baseline established that the hypernetwork approach works in bfloat16. Phase 2 adds quantization and verifies that the quality loss is acceptable (<10% Pass@1 degradation) — a distinct measurement from the core hypothesis.
      - heading "Deliverables" [level=3] [ref=e415]
      - list [ref=e416]:
        - listitem [ref=e417]:
          - text: "[ ]"
          - code [ref=e418]: libs/adapter-registry
          - text: ": SQLModel schema, write-once enforcement at the storage API level,"
          - code [ref=e419]: .safetensors
          - text: path resolution, adapter metadata queryable without loading weights into GPU memory
        - listitem [ref=e420]:
          - text: "[ ]"

          - text: "): Default to single-adapter retrieval at inference time. Multi-adapter composition (additive merging of heterogeneous LoRAs) is an optional future experiment, not the default architecture. The risk of interference in non-orthogonal weight subspaces is real; the single-adapter default avoids it."
        - paragraph [ref=e433]:
          - strong [ref=e434]: Catastrophic forgetting
          - text: (see
          - link "Risk Matrix" [ref=e435] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: ../appendices/risk-matrix/
          - text: "): Write-once semantics must be enforced at the registry API level, not as a convention. No code path may overwrite an existing adapter. Adapters are indexed by session ID and timestamp — if a new adapter is produced for the same task, it is a new entry, not an update."

        - strong [ref=e471]: "Evaluation method:"
        - text: Run the same held-out tasks with the Phase 1 adapter loaded into the Phase 2 vLLM lora-server, served in NF4 QLoRA mode. Compare Pass@1 between bfloat16 (Phase 1) and NF4 QLoRA (Phase 2).
      - paragraph [ref=e472]:

      - 'heading "Phase 3: Recursive Agent Loop and Sandbox Integration" [level=2] [ref=e475]'
      - paragraph [ref=e476]:
        - strong [ref=e477]: "Depends on:"
        - text: Phase 2 success criteria met.
      - paragraph [ref=e478]:
        - text: Contingent on Phase 2 delivering a functional adapter registry and serving layer, Phase 3 builds the core agent loop (
        - code [ref=e479]: services/rune-agent
        - text: ) and the sandboxed code execution environment. This phase produces the first end-to-end path from task to adapter — the complete recursive loop described in the architecture.
      - heading "Why This Phase Comes Here" [level=3] [ref=e480]
      - paragraph [ref=e481]: "Phase 3 depends on Phase 2 at three integration points: the agent needs the adapter registry to select adapters at session start, the lora-server to load and serve them, and the api-service extensions to query and store session state. Without Phase 2, the agent loop has nowhere to retrieve or store adapters. Building the agent loop before the serving infrastructure would require mocking the entire adapter layer — wasted work that needs to be undone."
      - paragraph [ref=e482]: Phase 3 also produces the adapter corpus required by Phase 4. The hypernetwork training job requires 50-100 diverse task-adapter pairs. The recursive agent loop is the mechanism that generates these pairs at scale. Phase 4 cannot begin until Phase 3 has produced sufficient corpus.
      - heading "Deliverables" [level=3] [ref=e483]
      - list [ref=e484]:
        - listitem [ref=e485]:
          - text: "[ ]"
          - code [ref=e486]: services/rune-agent
          - text: ": LangGraph"
          - code [ref=e487]: StateGraph
          - text: implementing
          - code [ref=e488]: generate → execute → reflect → save
          - text: cycle, with configurable maximum attempt count
        - listitem [ref=e489]: "[ ] Sandbox: Docker-based code execution with network isolation, memory limits, and CPU limits; agent operates outside the container"
        - listitem [ref=e490]: "[ ] Adapter selection: query adapter-registry at session start, load most-relevant adapter into lora-server based on task metadata"
        - listitem [ref=e491]:
          - text: "[ ]"
          - code [ref=e492]: libs/model-training
          - text: "extensions: PEFT utilities, trajectory-to-adapter fine-tuning script (direct LoRA fine-tuning path, distinct from hypernetwork inference)"
        - listitem [ref=e493]: "[ ] End-to-end test: task → agent loop → successful code → trajectory captured → passed to distillation → adapter stored in registry"
      - heading "Risk Callouts" [level=3] [ref=e494]
      - blockquote [ref=e495]:
        - paragraph [ref=e496]:
          - strong [ref=e497]: "Sandbox escape:"
          - text: Agent-generated code must not be able to reach the host network, host filesystem (outside the designated output directory), or other containers. Network isolation and read-only mounts are required from the first implementation — not added later as a hardening step.
        - paragraph [ref=e498]:
          - strong [ref=e499]: "Agent loop divergence:"
          - text: The generate-execute-reflect cycle requires a maximum attempt count. Without a hard stop, the loop retries indefinitely on tasks that are outside the model's current capability. The trajectory is still captured on loop termination (even without a passing solution) — a failed-but-attempted trajectory may still produce a useful adapter.

        - rowgroup [ref=e502]:

      - 'heading "Phase 4: Evolution Operator and Hypernetwork" [level=2] [ref=e529]'
      - paragraph [ref=e530]:
        - strong [ref=e531]: "Depends on:"
        - text: Phase 3 success criteria met AND adapter corpus of 50-100 diverse task-adapter pairs accumulated from Phase 3 runs.
      - paragraph [ref=e532]:
        - text: Contingent on Phase 3 delivering a functional recursive agent loop, Phase 4 builds the adapter lifecycle management system (
        - code [ref=e533]: services/evolution-svc
        - text: ) and the Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork inference path (
        - code [ref=e534]: services/training-svc
        - text: "hypernetwork job). This is the final phase and closes the loop: adapters produced by Phase 3 train the hypernetwork that generates future adapters."
      - heading "Why This Phase Comes Last" [level=3] [ref=e535]
      - paragraph [ref=e536]:
        - text: The hypernetwork requires a corpus of pre-trained adapters as training data — a cold-start problem that cannot be bypassed. Without Phase 3 producing diverse task-adapter pairs, the hypernetwork has nothing to train on. The evolution operator (
        - code [ref=e537]: services/evolution-svc
        - text: ") also depends on a populated adapter registry: fitness evaluation requires existing adapters to compare and promote."
      - paragraph [ref=e538]: Attempting to build Phase 4 before Phase 3 is complete would require synthetic adapter data, which may not reflect the distribution of real coding task adapters. The ordering is forced by the data dependency.
      - heading "Deliverables" [level=3] [ref=e539]
      - list [ref=e540]:
        - listitem [ref=e541]:
          - text: "[ ]"
          - code [ref=e542]: services/evolution-svc
          - text: ": fitness evaluation against held-out test sets, tournament selection, adapter pruning below fitness threshold, promotion of high-performing adapters in the hierarchy"
        - listitem [ref=e543]:
          - text: "[ ]"
          - code [ref=e544]: services/training-svc
          - text: ": hypernetwork training job (trains on Phase 3 adapter corpus), hypernetwork inference path (single forward pass → adapter weights, without gradient descent)"
        - listitem [ref=e545]: "[ ] Adapter hierarchy: project-root, domain, and task-specific levels populated by the evolution operator from Phase 3 adapters"
        - listitem [ref=e546]: "[ ] MLflow tracking: adapter fitness scores per evaluation run, evolution events (promotions, prunings, merges), hypernetwork reconstruction loss on held-out adapters"
      - heading "Risk Callouts" [level=3] [ref=e547]
      - blockquote [ref=e548]:
        - paragraph [ref=e549]:
          - strong [ref=e550]: Hypernetwork mode collapse
          - text: (see
          - link "Risk Matrix" [ref=e551] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: ../appendices/risk-matrix/
          - text: "): Diversity regularization must be in the training loss from the start of hypernetwork training. Monitor adapter cosine diversity at each checkpoint — if it falls below the threshold (< 0.1), stop training and investigate. The Phase 1 experiment will have provided a reference baseline for what healthy cosine diversity looks like."
        - paragraph [ref=e552]:
          - strong [ref=e553]: "Minimum corpus size:"
          - text: The hypernetwork training requires a diverse adapter corpus. Diversity here is over task types, failure modes, and correction patterns — not just adapter count. If the Phase 3 corpus lacks sufficient diversity (e.g., all adapters trained on similar tasks), delay hypernetwork training and continue accumulating adapters from a wider task distribution. The Phase 3 end-to-end test verifies individual adapters; this is a separate concern about corpus-level diversity.

      - paragraph [ref=e583]:
        - strong [ref=e584]: "Dataset:"
        - text: Held-out adapter subset from Phase 3 corpus (10-20% withheld from hypernetwork training set).
      - paragraph [ref=e585]:
        - strong [ref=e586]: "Baseline:"
        - text: Random adapter (Gaussian weight initialization at the same rank) as reconstruction loss reference; direct fine-tuned adapter Pass@1 as inference quality reference.
      - paragraph [ref=e587]:

        - text: Reconstruction loss (MSE between hypernetwork-generated adapter weights and held-out fine-tuned adapter weights); cosine diversity across a batch of hypernetwork-generated adapters; Pass@1 comparison on held-out HumanEval tasks using hypernetwork-generated vs fine-tuned adapters.
      - paragraph [ref=e589]:
        - strong [ref=e590]: "Expected range:"
        - text: Reconstruction loss below random baseline confirms the hypernetwork is learning the adapter manifold. Cosine diversity > 0.1 confirms diversity is preserved. Pass@1 parity with fine-tuned adapters (±5%) would be a strong result; 50-80% of fine-tuned adapter quality is the expected range for a first-pass hypernetwork.

content.thinking:
Now I see the issue. The mkdocstrings inheritance diagrams are generated with dotted class names like `evolution_svc.models.EvolutionJob` as node IDs, with the edge `-->` to connect parent classes. But the diagrams only show a single node because the parent class connections are missing.

Looking at the mermaid source:```
flowchart TD
evolution_svc.models.EvolutionJob[EvolutionJob]
```

There's only one node and no edges to parent classes. This means mkdocstrings is only generating the class itself without its parents (like `SQLModel`). This is actually a mkdocstrings issue - the inheritance diagram should show `SQLModel --> EvolutionJob` but it's only showing `EvolutionJob`.
 
So it seems mkdocstrings might intentionally exclude parent classes from external packages like `SQLModel` or `pydantic.BaseModel` when generating these diagrams, rather than this being a rendering problem with mermaid itself. The real issue is that these single-node diagrams aren't providing any value since all the classes only inherit from external libraries. I should recommend disabling `show_inheritance_diagram` to clean up the documentation, or explain that this is expected behavior and the diagrams won't show external base classes like `SQLModel` or `BaseModel`.

content.text:
Now I see the issue. These aren't the hand-written mermaid diagrams — they're **auto-generated inheritance diagrams from mkdocstrings**. The generated source is:

```
flowchart TD
evolution_svc.models.EvolutionJob[EvolutionJob]
```

Just one node, no edges. This is because mkdocstrings only includes project-documented classes in inheritance diagrams. Since `EvolutionJob` inherits from `SQLModel` (an external package), and `EvaluationRequest` from `BaseModel` (pydantic), there are no project-internal parents to show — so the diagram is always a single node.

This isn't a rendering bug. The simplest fix is to disable these unhelpful diagrams in the mkdocstrings config.

file.content:
- mkdocstrings:
    default_handler: python
    handlers:
      python:
        paths:
        - services/*/src
        - libs/*/src
        options:
          show_source: true
          show_root_heading: true
          show_category_heading: true
          show_signature_annotations: true
          show_bases: true
          heading_level: 2
          members_order: source
          docstring_style: google
          filters:
          - '!^_'
          merge_init_into_class: true
          show_if_no_docstring: false
          separate_signature: true
          signature_crossrefs: true
          show_submodules: true
          show_inheritance_diagram: true
          show_root_toc_entry: true

site_name: Rune Documentation

theme:
  name: mkdocs
  navigation_depth: 3
  locale: en
  logo: assets/et_illustration_transparent.png
